causeOfDeath: Sepsis infection triggered by a traumatic fall at a workplace.
familySituation: Survived by dependent widow.
methodologyNote: The appellate court strictly enforced the Ogden Tables, projecting the deceased's annual income until retirement age and applying a conventional two-thirds dependency fraction to calculate exact future financial loss.

causeOfDeath: Blunt force trauma sustained in a commercial motor vehicle accident.
familySituation: Survived by widow and three dependents.
methodologyNote: The tribunal applied the multiplier method to the victim's verified corporate salary of Rs. 47,250 per month, adding statutory allocations for loss of parental and filial consortium.

causeOfDeath: Crushing injuries sustained in a catastrophic train derailment.
familySituation: Not recorded in judgment.
methodologyNote: The award was dictated by the strict liability schedule under Section 124 of the Railways Act, which mandates a fixed maximum compensation of Rs. 8 lakhs for death regardless of the victim's age, income, or dependents.

causeOfDeath: Toxic epidermal necrolysis induced by a gross overdose of steroids administered during treatment for a viral fever.
familySituation: Survived by husband, an NRI medical doctor.
methodologyNote: The Supreme Court modified standard multiplier calculations to account for the victim's prospective income in the United States, using the award to financially penalise the hospital for systemic negligence.

causeOfDeath: Gangrene infection resulting from delayed emergency surgery following a severe leg injury.
familySituation: Survived by two parents.
methodologyNote: Because the student had no established income to apply a multiplier to, the commission doubled the lower tribunal's award by adding Rs. 10 lakhs explicitly categorised as compensation for the parents' lifelong mental agony.

causeOfDeath: Statistical probability of premature death from long-term exposure to environmental carcinogens.
familySituation: Not applicable. This is a regulatory figure, not a court payout.
methodologyNote: This is not a tort payout. The US EPA calculates this figure by aggregating willingness-to-pay data across large populations to justify the economic cost of life-saving federal regulations.

causeOfDeath: Accidental fall from a moving train.
familySituation: Not recorded in filing.
methodologyNote: The statutory framework mandates a minimal ex-gratia cash disbursement immediately upon proof of death on railway property, entirely divorced from any assessment of fault, economic loss, or tribunal litigation.

causeOfDeath: Incineration in an illegal industrial explosives facility.
familySituation: Not recorded in filing.
methodologyNote: The National Green Tribunal ordered an immediate standardised ex-gratia deposit by the District Magistrate pending the slower individualised calculations required under the Workmen's Compensation Act.

causeOfDeath: Lethal blunt trauma sustained while operating heavy factory machinery.
familySituation: Unmarried, living with parents.
methodologyNote: The tribunal awarded zero compensation because the strict statutory definitions required the parents to prove absolute financial dependency on the boy's Rs. 55 monthly wages, which they could not mathematically demonstrate.

caseName: Himanchal Kumari v. Govt. of NCT of Delhi
causeOfDeath: Death attributable to medical negligence at a government hospital.
occupation: Not recorded. Described as the sole financial breadwinner of the household.
familySituation: Deceased was the sole breadwinner; family entirely financially dependent on their income.
methodologyNote: Compensation awarded by a consumer disputes tribunal applying the pecuniary loss doctrine under Indian tort jurisprudence. The figure reflects the modest baseline for medical negligence payouts in India, even when the deceased was the primary income earner. Institutional caps and low-income multipliers heavily constrain the final award. Verify this: the specific year of judgment could not be confirmed through public search.

caseName: Shikha Garg v. The Boeing Company (Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302)
causeOfDeath: Crash of Boeing 737 MAX 8 (Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302) caused by defective MCAS flight-control software. All 157 aboard killed on 10 March 2019.
occupation: United Nations Development Programme environmental consultant and PhD candidate.
familySituation: Recently married; survived by husband Soumya Bhattacharya, who received a separate $3.45 million pre-trial settlement incorporated into the total.
methodologyNote: Chicago federal jury verdict of $28 million on 12 November 2025, the first civil trial among dozens of 737 MAX lawsuits. A same-day deal added 26% statutory interest and the husband's pre-trial component, bringing the final figure to $35.85 million. Boeing waived appeal rights. Critically, $10 million of the award was explicitly allocated by the jury for the six minutes of pre-impact terror, pain, and emotional distress experienced before the nosedive. Boeing had accepted liability prior to trial; the jury decided only damages.

caseName: Boeing 737 MAX Lion Air Flight 610 Out-of-Court Settlements
causeOfDeath: Crash of Boeing 737 MAX 8 (Lion Air Flight 610) into the Java Sea on 29 October 2018 due to defective MCAS flight-control software. All 189 aboard killed.
occupation: Varied; victims were predominantly Indonesian nationals.
familySituation: Varied. Settlement amounts scaled upward for victims with dependents (married with 1-3 children reportedly received $2-3 million).
methodologyNote: The $1.2 million figure is the confirmed minimum floor per family, first reported by Reuters in September 2019 when Wisner Law Firm announced 11 settled claims. Boeing did not admit liability in these settlements. Final amounts varied by victim nationality, age, income, marital status, and number of dependents. Indonesian incomes and prevailing award norms suppressed amounts significantly below what comparable US-victim settlements would have yielded. Boeing simultaneously ran a separate $100 million humanitarian assistance fund paying $144,500 per victim, independent of litigation rights.

caseName: September 11th Victim Compensation Fund (VCF I) - Highest Deceased Claim
causeOfDeath: Killed in the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks.
occupation: Elite financial sector professional (high-earning Wall Street or equivalent).
familySituation: Not recorded in public filings. Identity withheld by VCF confidentiality provisions.
methodologyNote: The VCF I (2001-2003), administered by Special Master Kenneth Feinberg, calculated each award as: non-economic loss + economic loss minus collateral source offsets. The highest single payout was driven entirely by the victim's projected lifetime financial sector earnings. No cap existed on total award amounts. This case illustrates the income-based valuation model's logical extreme: equal deaths produce radically unequal compensation based solely on prior earning trajectory. The exact figure may vary depending on source; verify against the official VCF final statistics report.

caseName: September 11th Victim Compensation Fund (VCF I) - Lowest Deceased Claim
causeOfDeath: Killed in the September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks.
occupation: Not recorded. Likely had modest income and substantial pre-existing private insurance or pension benefits.
familySituation: Not recorded in public filings.
methodologyNote: The anomalously low payout resulted from the VCF's mandatory collateral source offset provision, which required the fund to deduct all life insurance proceeds, pension benefits, and government benefits already received by the family. A victim with modest economic loss but above-average private insurance saw the collateral offset mathematically eliminate nearly the entire gross award, leaving a nominal residual. This is the documented minimum from VCF I. Verify this: some sources cite $87.02 as an even lower VCF II figure; the $4,121 applies specifically to the original 2001-2003 fund.

caseName: Union Carbide Corporation v. Union of India (Bhopal Gas Tragedy)
causeOfDeath: Mass exposure to methyl isocyanate (MIC) gas leak from Union Carbide India Limited plant on 2-3 December 1984. Estimated 3,000-15,000 deaths.
occupation: Varied; primarily low-income factory workers, slum residents, and Bhopal city inhabitants.
familySituation: Varied. Mass casualty event; survivors and bereaved families numbered in the hundreds of thousands.
methodologyNote: The Supreme Court of India approved a $470 million out-of-court settlement in February 1989. Distributed across an estimated 15,000-20,000 death claims and hundreds of thousands of injury claims, individual death payments averaged Rs. 1 lakh to Rs. 3 lakhs (here represented at the midpoint). The Government of India acting as parens patriae (sovereign representative) collapsed individual claims into a single aggregate, which paradoxically suppressed per-individual valuations. The settlement remains deeply controversial; litigation and claims processing continued for decades. The figure here is the documented average per-death disbursement, not a single adjudicated case.

caseName: Unnamed Medical Malpractice Settlement v. Bronx Lebanon Hospital
causeOfDeath: Death during a C-section (Caesarean delivery) as a result of surgical medical negligence.
familySituation: Not recorded in public filings. Settlement was confidential.
methodologyNote: Confidential out-of-court settlement. The $7 million figure represents the hospital's financial exposure under American medical malpractice liability standards, where the threat of a sympathetic jury in a Bronx court (historically one of the most plaintiff-friendly jurisdictions in the US) creates powerful incentives for pre-trial resolution. No public court record or docket entry is available due to the confidentiality agreement. Verify this: year and victim details could not be confirmed through public searches. The figure is as stated in the source document.

caseName: NGT Suo Motu Action on Harda Industrial Blast
causeOfDeath: Industrial explosion at an illegal or non-compliant explosives manufacturing facility in Harda, Madhya Pradesh.
familySituation: Not recorded in filing.
methodologyNote: The National Green Tribunal exercised suo motu (self-initiated) jurisdiction and ordered the District Magistrate to immediately deposit Rs. 15 lakhs per fatality as ex-gratia relief. This was a triage measure intended to quell public outrage and ensure immediate family support, explicitly separate from and pending the slower individualised calculations under the Workmen's Compensation Act. The standardised per-fatality deposit bypassed the usual fault-finding and means-assessment processes entirely.

caseName: Secretary, Department of Social Services v. Afford (Disability Care Regulatory Penalty)
causeOfDeath: Death of a patient with a disability while in the care of a registered NDIS or aged care provider, attributable to systemic care failures.
occupation: N/A. Victim was a disabled patient, not an employed worker.
familySituation: Not recorded in public filings.
methodologyNote: This is a civil regulatory penalty imposed on the care provider for systemic compliance failures, not a direct compensation award to the deceased's family. The $400,000 AUD fine treats the death as an administrative compliance violation under disability or aged care regulatory law. It illustrates how regulatory bodies in developed nations assign a financial cost to institutional negligence independently of any tort damages the family may separately pursue. Verify this: the exact year, jurisdiction (Federal Court or tribunal), and specific Act cited could not be independently confirmed through public search.

caseName: Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat - Cost-Benefit Analysis Guide: Value of a Statistical Life
causeOfDeath: N/A. This is a regulatory policy figure used in cost-benefit analysis for hypothetical lives saved by safety regulations, not a litigated case.
occupation: N/A. Represents a statistical average Canadian worker/citizen.
familySituation: N/A
methodologyNote: The Value of a Statistical Life (VSL) is a macroeconomic tool derived from revealed-preference studies (e.g., how much extra wage workers demand for risky jobs). The Canadian government uses $9.1 million CAD as the VSL when calculating the economic justification for safety infrastructure investments. This figure is categorically distinct from what courts award in individual tort cases, where income-based formulas produce far lower numbers. The contrast between the VSL and actual tort payouts exposes the structural gap between what a government claims a life is worth when justifying preventive spending versus what it awards in reactive litigation.

caseName: Office of Best Practice Regulation (OBPR) Guidance - Value of a Statistical Life
causeOfDeath: N/A. Regulatory policy figure for hypothetical lives saved by safety regulations, not a litigated case.
occupation: N/A. Represents a statistical average Australian citizen.
familySituation: N/A
methodologyNote: The Australian Government's OBPR sets the VSL at $5.87 million AUD for use in regulatory impact statements, representing the aggregate willingness-to-pay across the population for a marginal reduction in mortality risk. As with the Canadian VSL, this figure significantly exceeds what Australian courts award in wrongful death or negligence cases to individual families, creating a normative tension between proactive governmental valuation and reactive judicial compensation.

caseName: Harris Powers & Cunningham Maricopa County Medical Malpractice Verdict (Wrongful Death of Stay-at-Home Mother)
causeOfDeath: Death of a stay-at-home mother due to medical negligence, cause of death not further specified in public records.
occupation: Stay-at-home mother with no formal salary or employment income.
familySituation: Survived by minor dependent children. The verdict explicitly valued unpaid household labor, childcare, and domestic services.
methodologyNote: Maricopa County (Arizona) jury verdict obtained by Harris, Powers & Cunningham. The significance of this case is its direct challenge to the traditional pecuniary loss doctrine (which penalises victims without formal income). The jury recognised the full economic replacement value of unpaid domestic and childcare labour — i.e., the cost of hiring nannies, housekeepers, and domestic managers to replicate all services the deceased provided. This approach counters the historic legal bias that treats homemakers as economically worthless. Verify this: the specific case name and year could not be independently confirmed; the $5.875 million figure is sourced from the firm's published case results page.

caseName: Co-op Legal Services Wrongful Death Settlement (Missed Cancer Diagnosis)
causeOfDeath: Death caused by late-stage cancer, where the cancer was not diagnosed at a clinically actionable stage due to medical negligence.
familySituation: Not recorded in public filings. Settlement was confidential.
methodologyNote: Out-of-court settlement facilitated by Co-op Legal Services. Under English and Welsh law, wrongful death claims are governed by the Fatal Accidents Act 1976, which limits recovery primarily to dependency claims (financial loss to dependants) and a fixed bereavement award. The absence of US-style pain and suffering multipliers and punitive damages structurally caps UK wrongful death settlements. The family accepted a lower certain sum rather than face the cost, delay, and emotional burden of a contested trial. Verify this: the specific year, court, and victim details could not be confirmed through public searches.

caseName: Thomas v. Burack (New York Medical Malpractice Verdict)
causeOfDeath: Death or catastrophic injury attributable to medical negligence, specific facts not publicly recorded.
familySituation: Not recorded.
methodologyNote: Verify this: the case name 'Thomas v. Burack' could not be independently confirmed through public legal databases or news archives. The approximate $19-20 million figure is consistent with the range of New York jury verdicts for catastrophic medical malpractice claims and reflects the uncapped, punitive character of American medical malpractice awards. New York juries historically treat large verdicts as mechanisms for institutional accountability, not just individual compensation. The specific payout figure should be verified against primary court records before publishing.

caseName: Marhunova v. OCF Construction LLC et al. (Philadelphia Court of Common Pleas, Case ID: 220501520)
causeOfDeath: Fatal fall of approximately 50 feet from an improperly secured fifth-floor balcony at a luxury townhouse construction site in Fitler Square, Philadelphia, on 15 December 2021. Victim fell through a defective temporary wooden guardrail.
occupation: Construction worker (siding installer), subcontractor on a residential development site.
familySituation: Survived by widow Hanna Marhunova and a 3-year-old son. Family had recently immigrated to Philadelphia from Belarus.
methodologyNote: Philadelphia jury verdict returned 27 June 2024, upheld by Judge Angelo Foglietta on post-trial motion (December 2024). Jury apportioned liability: OCF Construction 50%, Fitler Construction Group 20%, 2330 Sansom Street LLC 20%, subcontractors 10%. IMPORTANT DISCREPANCY: The source document states '$37.5 million' — this figure is incorrect based on all available public records. The actual total jury award was $68.5 million. The $37.5 million figure may reflect OCF's 50% liability share (~$34.25 million) or a drafting error in the source document. The full $68.5 million figure is used here. Multiple defendants settled before trial; the verdict covered the estate and family as individual claimants. OSHA violations were central to the liability finding.
